> **Formal Definition:** A *hydraulic motor* is a rotary mechanical device that converts hydraulic pressure energy (from a pressurized fluid, typically oil at **70–210 bar**) into rotational mechanical energy (torque and angular velocity).

- Used in **heavy-payload industrial robots** (e.g., Unimate 2000B, Cincinnati Milacron T3).
- High power-to-weight ratio (~**5 kW/kg**).
- **Stiffness**: Excellent – ideal for precision machining robots.

**Analogy:** A hydraulic motor is like the steering system of a JCB excavator – a small movement of the joystick (control valve) produces enormous digging force at the bucket.

---

### 1.4 DC Motor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A *DC motor* is an electromechanical energy converter that transforms DC electrical energy into mechanical rotational energy using the interaction of current-carrying conductors with a magnetic field, governed by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.

- The **back-EMF constant (K_e)** and **torque constant (K_t)** are related as: **K_e = K_t** (in SI units).
- Speed range: **0–6000 rpm** (typical industrial).
- Efficiency: **60–75%** for standard DC motors.

**Analogy:** A DC motor is like a ceiling fan – apply voltage, it spins; reduce voltage, it slows down; reverse voltage, it reverses direction.

---

### 1.5 Stepper Motor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A *stepper motor* is a brushless, synchronous electric motor that divides a full rotation into a discrete number of equal steps, with the shaft's angular position locked at each step, allowing precise open-loop position control without feedback encoders.

- Step angles: **1.8°** (standard, 200 steps/rev), **0.72°**, or **0.9°**.
- Used in low-cost positioning robots, CNC, 3D printers.

**Analogy:** A stepper motor is like a clock's second hand – it moves in tiny, precise, countable ticks. Tell it to move "50 ticks clockwise," and it does exactly that, even without a position sensor.

---

### 1.6 Power Transmission Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Power transmission systems* in robotics are mechanical elements that transmit motion and torque from the drive (motor shaft) to the robot joint while modifying speed, torque, and direction.

Common types:
1. **Gear trains** (spur, helical, planetary, harmonic)
2. **Belts and pulleys** (timing belts)
3. **Chain drives**
4. **Lead screws / ball screws** (linear transmission)
5. **Direct-drive coupling**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Role:** A 6-DOF industrial robot arm weighing 100 kg may use harmonic-drive reducers with ratios of **100:1 to 200:1**, multiplying motor torque by the same factor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pitch and Yaw – Rotational Analysis

The orientation of a rigid body in 3D space can be described by **three sequential elementary rotations**. For pitch (θ) and yaw (ψ), the elementary rotation matrices about the principal axes are:

#### Step-by-Step Logic:
- A point P in space has coordinates (x, y, z).
- Rotating it by angle θ about Y-axis changes x and z but keeps y fixed → **Pitch**.
- Rotating by angle ψ about Z-axis changes x and y but keeps z fixed → **Yaw**.

#### Rotation Matrices (Right-Hand Rule Convention)

**Pitch rotation (about Y-axis by angle θ):**

$$
R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Yaw rotation (about Z-axis by angle ψ):**

$$
R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Roll rotation (about X-axis by angle φ) – for completeness:**

$$
R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Composite Orientation (Z-Y-X Euler Convention – KTU standard)

$$
R = R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)
$$

This product gives the complete orientation matrix of the end-effector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Use:** In robot kinematics, when the controller specifies "move end-effector to (x, y, z) with orientation (ψ, θ, φ)," the inverse-kinematics solver uses these matrices to compute individual joint angles for the six servo motors of a typical industrial arm.

---

### 2.2 DC Motor – Mathematical Model

#### Logical Breakdown:
1. **Electrical side:** Applied voltage V drives current I through armature resistance R and inductance L, opposed by back-EMF E.
2. **Magnetic coupling:** Current I produces torque T proportional to flux and current.
3. **Mechanical side:** Torque T accelerates the rotor (J) against friction (B) and load (T_L).

#### Voltage Equation

$$
V = E + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}
$$

In steady state (constant speed):

$$
V = K_e \omega + I_a R_a
$$

#### Torque Equation

$$
T = K_t I_a
$$

#### Mechanical (Newton's) Equation

$$
T - T_L - B\omega = J \frac{d\omega}{dt}
$$

#### Steady-State Speed Equation

$$
\omega = \frac{V}{K_e} - \frac{R_a}{(K_e K_t)} T_L
$$

---

### 2.3 Stepper Motor – Step Angle and Resolution

#### Step Angle Formula

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}
$$

Where:
- $N_s$ = Number of stator teeth (poles)
- $N_r$ = Number of rotor teeth
- $\theta_s$ = Step angle in degrees

#### Resolution (Steps per Revolution)

$$
\text{Steps/rev} = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = N_s \cdot N_r
$$

#### Microstepping Factor

For a microstepping driver with subdivision factor $\mu$:

$$
\theta_{\text{micro}} = \frac{\theta_s}{\mu}
$$

---

### 2.4 Hydraulic Motor – Power & Torque Relations

#### Volumetric & Mechanical Power

$$
P_{\text{hydraulic}} = \Delta P \cdot Q
$$

$$
P_{\text{mechanical}} = T \cdot \omega
$$

#### Hydraulic Motor Torque

$$
T = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_d}{2\pi} \cdot \eta_m
$$

Where:
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure differential (Pa)
- $V_d$ = Displacement volume per revolution (m³/rev)
- $\eta_m$ = Mechanical efficiency (~**0.90–0.95**)

#### Hydraulic Motor Speed

$$
\omega = \frac{Q}{V_d} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \eta_v
$$

Where $\eta_v$ = Volumetric efficiency (~**0.95–0.98**).

#### Practical Hydraulic Formulas (Industry Units)

$$
T \text{ (Nm)} = \frac{P \text{ (bar)} \times V_d \text{ (cc/rev)}}{62.8} \times \eta_m
$$

$$
\omega \text{ (rpm)} = \frac{Q \text{ (l/min)} \times 1000}{V_d \text{ (cc/rev)}} \times \eta_v
$$

---

### 2.5 Power Transmission Systems – Gear & Belt Math

#### Gear Train Ratio

$$
i = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 \cdot \eta}
$$

Where:
- $N_1$, $N_2$ = Number of teeth on driver/driven gear
- $\omega_1$, $\omega_2$ = Angular speeds
- $T_1$, $T_2$ = Torques
- $\eta$ = Transmission efficiency (gear ≈ 0.95, belt ≈ 0.97, harmonic ≈ 0.85)

#### Belt Drive Ratio

$$
i = \frac{D_2}{D_1}
$$

Where $D_1$, $D_2$ = Pitch diameters of driver/driven pulleys.

#### Compound (Multi-Stage) Gear Train

$$
i_{\text{total}} = \prod_{k=1}^{n} \frac{N_{\text{driven},k}}{N_{\text{driving},k}}
$$

#### Power Transmitted

$$
P = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \text{ (W)}
$$

Where N is speed in rpm.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Comprehensive)

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Pitch rotation matrix | $R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta\\ 0 & 1 & 0\\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$ | θ in **radians** |
| Yaw rotation matrix | $R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0\\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0\\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ | ψ in **radians** |
| Composite RPY orientation | $R = R_z(\psi)\,R_y(\theta)\,R_x(\phi)$ | Standard **ZYX** convention |
| DC motor back-EMF | $E = K_e \,\omega$ | $K_e$ in **V·s/rad** |
| DC motor torque | $T = K_t\,I_a$ | $K_t$ in **Nm/A** |
| DC motor steady speed | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e}$ | ω in **rad/s** |
| Stepper step angle | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s N_r}$ | $\theta_s$ in **degrees** |
| Stepper microstep | $\theta_{\mu} = \dfrac{\theta_s}{\mu}$ | μ = microstep divisor |
| Hydraulic torque | $T = \dfrac{\Delta P\, V_d}{2\pi}\,\eta_m$ | $\Delta P$ in **Pa**, $V_d$ in **m³/rev** |
| Hydraulic speed | $\omega = \dfrac{Q}{V_d}\,2\pi\,\eta_v$ | Q in **m³/s** |
| Hydraulic power | $P = \Delta P\,Q = T\,\omega$ | P in **Watts** |
| Gear ratio | $i = N_2/N_1 = \omega_1/\omega_2$ | Dimensionless |
| Torque amplification | $T_2 = T_1 \,i\,\eta$ | $\eta$ in **[0, 1]** |
| Mechanical power | $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ | N in **rpm**, T in **Nm** |
| Belt-drive ratio | $i = D_2/D_1$ | D = pitch **diameter** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Composite Rotation Matrix for Pitch + Yaw (RPY)

**Problem:** Derive the complete orientation matrix R that represents a rotation of $\psi$ about Z-axis followed by $\theta$ about Y-axis.

#### Step 1 – Yaw Rotation About Z-axis

A yaw rotation by angle ψ acts on a point (x, y, z):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x\cos\psi - y\sin\psi \\
y' &= x\sin\psi + y\cos\psi \\
z' &= z
\end{aligned}
$$

In matrix form (applied to column vector $[x\ y\ z]^T$):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ z' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $R_z(\psi)$ is identified.

#### Step 2 – Pitch Rotation About Y-axis

A pitch rotation by angle θ acts on (x, y, z):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x'' &= x\cos\theta + z\sin\theta \\
y'' &= y \\
z'' &= -x\sin\theta + z\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x'' \\ y'' \\ z'' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ z' \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $R_y(\theta)$ is identified.

#### Step 3 – Sequential Composition

Performing yaw first, then pitch (passive transformation convention):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x'' \\ y'' \\ z'' \end{bmatrix} = R_y(\theta)\, R_z(\psi) \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 4 – Explicit Multiplication

$$
R_y(\theta)\, R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing each entry (row × column):

- **Row 1, Col 1:** $\cos\theta \cos\psi + \sin\theta \cdot 0 = \cos\theta\cos\psi$
- **Row 1, Col 2:** $\cos\theta(-\sin\psi) + \sin\theta \cdot 0 = -\cos\theta\sin\psi$
- **Row 1, Col 3:** $\cos\theta \cdot 0 + \sin\theta \cdot 1 = \sin\theta$
- **Row 2, Col 1:** $0\cdot\cos\psi + 1\cdot\sin\psi + 0 = \sin\psi$
- **Row 2, Col 2:** $0\cdot(-\sin\psi) + 1\cdot\cos\psi + 0 = \cos\psi$
- **Row 2, Col 3:** $0$
- **Row 3, Col 1:** $-\sin\theta\cos\psi + 0 + \cos\theta \cdot 0 = -\sin\theta\cos\psi$
- **Row 3, Col 2:** $-\sin\theta(-\sin\psi) + 0 + \cos\theta \cdot 0 = \sin\theta\sin\psi$
- **Row 3, Col 3:** $-\sin\theta \cdot 0 + 0 + \cos\theta \cdot 1 = \cos\theta$

#### Step 5 – Final Composite Matrix

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta\cos\psi & -\cos\theta\sin\psi & \sin\theta \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ -\sin\theta\cos\psi & \sin\theta\sin\psi & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** This matrix is what an industrial robot's controller computes internally to verify whether the tool's orientation matches the target orientation in Cartesian space (used in path-planning for arc welding and laser cutting).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: DC Motor Steady-State Speed vs. Torque Characteristic

**Problem:** Derive the relationship between motor speed ω and load torque $T_L$ for a separately-excited DC motor.

#### Step 1 – KVL Around the Armature Circuit

$$
V = E + I_a R_a \quad \text{(steady state, } L_a\frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0 \text{)}
$$

#### Step 2 – Substitute Back-EMF Expression

$$
V = K_e \omega + I_a R_a
$$

#### Step 3 – Solve for Armature Current

$$
I_a = \frac{V - K_e \omega}{R_a}
$$

#### Step 4 – Express Torque (from electromagnetic torque equation)

$$
T = K_t I_a
$$

#### Step 5 – Substitute Current into Torque Equation

$$
T = K_t \left(\frac{V - K_e \omega}{R_a}\right)
$$

#### Step 6 – Solve for Speed ω as a Function of Torque T

$$
T R_a = K_t (V - K_e \omega)
$$

$$
\frac{T R_a}{K_t} = V - K_e \omega
$$

$$
K_e \omega = V - \frac{T R_a}{K_t}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\omega = \frac{V}{K_e} - \frac{R_a}{K_e K_t} \, T\;}
$$

#### Step 7 – Interpretation (No-Load Speed & Stall Torque)

- **No-load speed** (T = 0): $\omega_0 = V/K_e$
- **Stall torque** (ω = 0): $T_{stall} = K_t V / R_a$

The speed-torque curve is a **straight line** with negative slope, slope = $-R_a/(K_e K_t)$.

#### Numerical Validation

A 24 V DC motor with $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad, $R_a = 1.2$ Ω, $K_t = 0.05$ Nm/A:

- $\omega_0 = 24/0.05 = 480$ rad/s ≈ **4585 rpm**
- $T_{stall} = 0.05 \times 24 / 1.2 = 1.0$ Nm

If load torque = 0.5 Nm:

$$
\omega = 480 - \frac{1.2}{0.05 \times 0.05}(0.5) = 480 - 240 = 240 \text{ rad/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** This linear speed-torque relation is the foundation of servo sizing in robotics. It allows engineers to pick a motor whose torque curve intersects the load's required operating point with margin for acceleration transients.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Stepper Motor Holding Torque Requirement

**Problem:** A robot joint requires a holding torque of **2.5 Nm** at standstill to resist gravity. A stepper motor with holding torque 1.2 Nm is available, with a 5:1 reduction gear. Is the configuration adequate if efficiency = 0.85?

#### Step 1 – Effective Output Torque from Motor + Gear

$$
T_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{motor}} \times i \times \eta
$$

#### Step 2 – Substitute Values

$$
T_{\text{out}} = 1.2 \times 5 \times 0.85
$$

$$
T_{\text{out}} = 5.1 \text{ Nm}
$$

#### Step 3 – Compare to Requirement

$$
T_{\text{out}} = 5.1 \text{ Nm} \;>\; T_{\text{required}} = 2.5 \text{ Nm} \;\; \checkmark
$$

#### Step 4 – Margin Factor

$$
\text{Safety Factor} = \frac{5.1}{2.5} = 2.04
$$

A safety factor > **2.0** is considered excellent for industrial robot joints (acceptable range: 1.5–2.5).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Takeaway:** The gear-head not only multiplies torque but also multiplies inertia (in reflected sense), affecting the dynamic response. Engineers must verify both static holding capability **and** dynamic acceleration capability.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Hydraulic Motor Sizing for a Robot Joint

**Problem:** A 6-DOF hydraulic robot requires a joint torque of **150 Nm** at speed **30 rpm**. Supply pressure = **140 bar**, return pressure = **5 bar**. Determine the required motor displacement and flow rate.

#### Step 1 – Effective Pressure Differential

$$
\Delta P = P_{\text{supply}} - P_{\text{return}} = 140 - 5 = 135 \text{ bar} = 13.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

#### Step 2 – Required Displacement Volume (using $T = \Delta P \cdot V_d / (2\pi) \cdot \eta_m$)

Assume $\eta_m = 0.90$:

$$
V_d = \frac{T \cdot 2\pi}{\Delta P \cdot \eta_m}
$$

$$
V_d = \frac{150 \times 2\pi}{13.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.90}
$$

$$
V_d = \frac{942.48}{12.15 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
V_d = 7.76 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 77.6 \text{ cc/rev}
$$

#### Step 3 – Required Flow Rate

$$
\omega = 30 \text{ rpm} = \frac{30 \times 2\pi}{60} = \pi \text{ rad/s} = 3.1416 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{V_d \cdot \omega}{2\pi \cdot \eta_v}
$$

Assuming $\eta_v = 0.95$:

$$
Q = \frac{77.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.1416}{2\pi \times 0.95}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{2.438 \times 10^{-4}}{5.969}
$$

$$
Q = 4.084 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 40.84 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} = 2.45 \text{ l/min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For 6-DOF robots, each joint motor may consume 2–10 l/min depending on size. A complete hydraulic power unit (HPU) typically delivers **40–80 l/min** at **200 bar**, with a **7.5–22 kW electric motor** driving the pump. This is why hydraulic robots are used in foundries, forges, and large-part handling where electric motors would overheat.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Gear Train Speed Reduction & Torque Amplification

**Problem:** A DC motor delivers 2 Nm at 3000 rpm. It drives a 3-stage gear train with ratios 5:1, 4:1, and 3:1. Each stage has efficiency 0.95. Find output speed, torque, and power.

#### Step 1 – Total Gear Ratio

$$
i_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 4 \times 3 = 60
$$

#### Step 2 – Output Speed

$$
\omega_{\text{out}} = \frac{\omega_{\text{in}}}{i_{\text{total}}} = \frac{3000}{60} = 50 \text{ rpm}
$$

#### Step 3 – Overall Efficiency (Series Multiplication)

$$
\eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_3 = 0.95 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 = 0.857
$$

#### Step 4 – Output Torque

$$
T_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{in}} \times i_{\text{total}} \times \eta_{\text{total}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{out}} = 2 \times 60 \times 0.857 = 102.9 \text{ Nm}
$$

#### Step 5 – Power Verification

$$
P_{\text{in}} = T_{\text{in}} \cdot \omega_{\text{in}} = 2 \times \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 2 \times 314.16 = 628.32 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{out}} \cdot \omega_{\text{out}} = 102.9 \times \frac{2\pi \times 50}{60} = 102.9 \times 5.236 = 538.79 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} \times \eta_{\text{total}} = 628.32 \times 0.857 = 538.5 \text{ W} \;\; \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** This trade-off (high speed in → low speed out, high torque out → low torque in) is exactly what robot joints need: small, fast motors combined with large reduction gears deliver the high-torque, low-speed actuation required for precise positioning under payload.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Roll-Pitch-Yaw Orientation Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Robot End Effector Reference Frame] --> B[Roll phi about X axis]
    A --> C[Pitch theta about Y axis]
    A --> D[Yaw psi about Z axis]
    B --> E[Combined Orientation Matrix R]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Position in 3D Cartesian Space]
    F --> G[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    G --> H[Six Joint Servo Commands]
    H --> I[Robot Arm Motion]
```

### 4.2 Drive Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Joint Drives] --> B[Electric Drives]
    A --> C[Hydraulic Drives]
    A --> D[Pneumatic Drives]
    B --> B1[DC Motor]
    B --> B2[Stepper Motor]
    B --> B3[Servo Motor]
    B --> B4[BLDC Motor]
    C --> C1[Gear Type Hydraulic Motor]
    C --> C2[Vane Type Hydraulic Motor]
    C --> C3[Piston Type Hydraulic Motor]
    D --> D1[Pneumatic Cylinder]
    D --> D2[Air Motor]
```

### 4.3 DC Motor Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Voltage Source V] --> B[Armature Resistance Ra]
    B --> C[Back EMF Generator Ke omega]
    C --> D[Mechanical Output Shaft]
    D --> E[Torque Generator Kt Ia]
    E --> F[Load Torque TL]
    D --> G[Moment of Inertia J]
    D --> H[Viscous Friction B]
```

### 4.4 Stepper Motor Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Microcontroller or Pulse Generator] --> B[Stepper Driver IC]
    B --> C[Phase Sequencer]
    C --> D1[Coil A Positive]
    C --> D2[Coil A Negative]
    C --> D3[Coil B Positive]
    C --> D4[Coil B Negative]
    D1 --> E[Rotor Movement theta s per pulse]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Output Shaft Position]
```

### 4.5 Hydraulic Drive System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electric Motor 7 to 22 kW] --> B[Hydraulic Pump]
    B --> C[Reservoir and Filter]
    C --> D[Pressure Relief Valve 200 bar]
    D --> E[Direction Control Valve]
    E --> F[Servo or Proportional Valve]
    F --> G[Hydraulic Motor at Joint]
    G --> H[Output Torque T]
    G --> I[Return Line to Reservoir]
```

### 4.6 Power Transmission System Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Drive Motor Shaft] --> B{Transmission Type}
    B --> C1[Spur or Helical Gear]
    B --> C2[Planetary Gear]
    B --> C3[Harmonic Drive]
    B --> C4[Timing Belt]
    B --> C5[Lead or Ball Screw]
    B --> C6[Chain Drive]
    C1 --> D[Output Joint Shaft]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E[Robot Link Motion]
```

### 4.7 Robot Drive Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Joint Requirement] --> B{Payload Size}
    B -->|Less than 5 kg| C[Stepper or Small Servo]
    B -->|5 to 50 kg| D[DC Servo with Harmonic Drive]
    B -->|50 to 500 kg| E[Hydraulic Motor]
    B -->|Greater than 500 kg| F[Heavy Hydraulic or Hybrid]
    C --> G[Low Cost Precision]
    D --> H[Industrial Standard]
    E --> I[High Power Density]
    F --> J[Aerospace or Heavy Industry]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Define pitch and yaw in the context of robot wrist orientation. State the corresponding axes of rotation.*

**Model Answer:**
Pitch and yaw are two rotational degrees of freedom of a robot's wrist joint, used to orient the end-effector. **Pitch (θ)** is the rotation of the end-effector about the **Y-axis** (lateral axis), tilting the tool up or down. **Yaw (ψ)** is the rotation about the **Z-axis** (vertical axis), swinging the tool left or right. Together with roll (about X-axis), they form the RPY convention for complete 3D orientation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between a DC motor and a stepper motor used in robotic drives.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DC Motor | Stepper Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Requires encoder/tachometer for closed-loop control | Operates in open-loop with discrete steps |
| Step resolution | Continuous rotation | Discrete steps (typically 1.8°) |
| Holding torque at standstill | Near zero without feedback | High inherent detent torque |
| Cost | Higher (with controller + encoder) | Lower (simpler drive electronics) |
| Application | High-speed continuous motion (welding arm) | Low-speed precision positioning (pick-and-place) |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of rotation matrices, derive the orientation matrix R for a robot end-effector subjected to a yaw of ψ = 30° about the Z-axis followed by a pitch of θ = 45° about the Y-axis. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A DC motor has the following specifications: $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 1.0$ Ω, $K_e = 1.2$ V·s/rad, $K_t = 1.2$ Nm/A. The motor is driving a robotic joint with a load torque of 8 Nm. Determine the steady-state armature current, speed (in rpm), and developed power. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Section 3.1 derivation. Substituting ψ = 30°, θ = 45°:

- cos 30° = √3/2 ≈ 0.8660
- sin 30° = 0.5
- cos 45° = sin 45° = √2/2 ≈ 0.7071

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7071 \times 0.8660 & -0.7071 \times 0.5 & 0.7071 \\ 0.5 & 0.8660 & 0 \\ -0.7071 \times 0.8660 & 0.7071 \times 0.5 & 0.7071 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6124 & -0.3536 & 0.7071 \\ 0.5000 & 0.8660 & 0 \\ -0.6124 & 0.3536 & 0.7071 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matrix fully describes the orientation of the end-effector relative to the base frame after applying yaw-then-pitch rotations.

**(b)** Solving:

**Step 1 – Armature Current:**
$$
T = K_t I_a \;\Rightarrow\; I_a = T/K_t = 8/1.2 = 6.667 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 – Back-EMF:**
$$
E = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 6.667 \times 1.0 = 213.33 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 – Speed:**
$$
\omega = E/K_e = 213.33 / 1.2 = 177.78 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Converting to rpm:
$$
N = \omega \times 60 / (2\pi) = 177.78 \times 60 / 6.2832 = 1697.7 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 4 – Developed Power:**
$$
P = T \cdot \omega = 8 \times 177.78 = 1422.2 \text{ W} \approx 1.42 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** $I_a = 6.67$ A, $N = 1698$ rpm, $P = 1.42$ kW.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a stepper motor. A stepper motor has 50 stator teeth and 50 rotor teeth. Calculate its step angle and the number of steps required for one full revolution. If a microstepping driver with μ = 16 is used, what is the effective step angle? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic motor with displacement 100 cc/rev operates at a pressure of 160 bar with a flow rate of 30 l/min. Assuming mechanical efficiency ηm = 0.92 and volumetric efficiency ηv = 0.96, determine the output torque and rotational speed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle:**
A stepper motor operates on the principle of **electromagnetic attraction/repulsion** between sequentially energized stator poles and a toothed permanent-magnet or variable-reluctance rotor. Each electrical pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angular increment (the step angle). The rotor locks at each step position, providing inherent position-holding capability without external feedback.

**Step Angle Calculation:**
$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r} = \frac{360°}{50 \times 50} = \frac{360°}{2500} = 0.144°
$$

**Steps per Revolution:**
$$
\text{Steps/rev} = 1/0.144° \times 360° = 2500 \text{ steps}
$$

**Microstep Angle:**
$$
\theta_{\text{micro}} = \theta_s / \mu = 0.144° / 16 = 0.009°
$$

**(b) Hydraulic Motor Solution:**

**Step 1 – Effective Pressure Differential:**
$$
\Delta P = 160 \text{ bar} = 160 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.6 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 – Output Torque:**
$$
T = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_d}{2\pi} \cdot \eta_m
$$

$$
T = \frac{1.6 \times 10^7 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{2\pi} \times 0.92
$$

$$
T = \frac{1600}{6.2832} \times 0.92 = 254.65 \times 0.92 = 234.3 \text{ Nm}
$$

**Step 3 – Flow Rate Conversion:**
$$
Q = 30 \text{ l/min} = 30 \times 10^{-3} / 60 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 – Rotational Speed:**
$$
\omega = \frac{Q}{V_d} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \eta_v
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{100 \times 10^{-6}} \times 2\pi \times 0.96
$$

$$
\omega = 5 \times 6.2832 \times 0.96 = 30.16 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
N = \omega \times 60 / (2\pi) = 30.16 \times 60 / 6.2832 = 288 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T = 234.3$ Nm, $N = 288$ rpm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing bar/Pa, l/min/m³/s, and cc/m³. Always convert to SI before substituting into formulas.
> 2. **Rotation matrix sign convention:** Use the **right-hand rule**. A common mistake is reversing the sign of sine terms in $R_y(\theta)$.
> 3. **Neglecting efficiency:** In gear-train problems, if you omit $\eta$, you will lose 2–3 marks. KTU examiners explicitly test whether students understand power losses.
> 4. **Confusing K_e and K_t units:** $K_e$ is in V·s/rad (or V/krpm for non-SI), while $K_t$ is in Nm/A. In SI, their numerical values are equal, but their units differ.
> 5. **Stepper motor formula confusion:** Step angle depends on the **product** $N_s \cdot N_r$, not their sum or ratio.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pitch (θ):** Rotation about **Y-axis**; nods the tool up/down. Matrix $R_y(\theta)$.
- **Yaw (ψ):** Rotation about **Z-axis**; swings tool left/right. Matrix $R_z(\psi)$.
- **Roll (φ):** Rotation about **X-axis**; banks the tool. Matrix $R_x(\phi)$.
- **Drives:** Convert energy into joint motion. Three classes: **electric, hydraulic, pneumatic**.
- **DC Motor:** Continuous rotation; speed controlled by voltage, torque by current. Uses $V = K_e\omega + I_a R_a$ and $T = K_t I_a$.
- **Stepper Motor:** Discrete steps; no feedback needed. Step angle = $360°/(N_s N_r)$. Open-loop precision.
- **Hydraulic Motor:** Converts pressurized fluid into rotation. Best for high-payload robots. $T = \Delta P \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_m / (2\pi)$.
- **Power Transmission:**
  - **Gear trains** amplify torque by ratio $i = N_2/N_1$.
  - **Efficiency multiplied** in series stages: $\eta_{\text{total}} = \prod \eta_k$.
  - **Belt drives** use ratio $i = D_2/D_1$.
  - **Harmonic drives** provide very high ratios (100:1+) with near-zero backlash.
- **Speed-Torque Trade-off:** Gear reduction reduces speed by factor $i$ but increases torque by $i \cdot \eta$.
- **Sizing Rule of Thumb:** Motor rated torque should be ≥ **1.5–2×** the steady-state joint torque for safe dynamic operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position Sensor, Potentiometer, Encoders, LVDT, Velocity Sensor, Acceleration Sensor, Force, Pressure and Torque Sensor, Touch and Tactile Sensor, Proximity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT601 - ROBOTICS & MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Position Sensor, Potentiometer, Encoders, LVDT, Velocity Sensor, Acceleration Sensor, Force, Pressure and Torque Sensor, Touch and Tactile Sensor, Proximity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1. Introduction to Sensors in Robotics

In the architecture of a modern robotic manipulator, **sensors** function as the peripheral nervous system. While the controller acts as the brain and the actuators act as the muscles, sensors are responsible for perceiving the internal state of the robot (proprioception) and the external state of the environment (exteroception). Module 2 of PCMRT601 deals exhaustively with these transducers, which convert physical phenomena—displacement, velocity, force, or proximity—into electrical signals that the controller can interpret.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 2024 scheme, the focus is on the **operating principle, mathematical model, characteristic equation, and typical application** of each sensor family. Memorizing only definitions will not yield full marks; derivations and circuit interpretations are high-weightage.

---

## 2. Position Sensors

A **position sensor** is a device that detects the displacement of an object—whether linear (along a single axis) or angular (rotary)—and converts it into a quantifiable electrical output (voltage, current, or digital pulse).

### 2.1 Potentiometer

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A potentiometer is a passive, three-terminal **variable resistor** that operates on the principle of **voltage division**. As a wiper slides along a resistive track, the output voltage is directly proportional to the mechanical displacement of the wiper.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a water tap. The further you turn the handle, the more water flows. A potentiometer is the electrical twin of this concept: the further the slider moves, the more of the input voltage appears at the output. In a robotic elbow joint, rotating the joint also rotates the knob of a potentiometer, instantly giving the controller a voltage reading equivalent to the joint angle.

- **Standard Metric:** Resolution is determined by the **wire-wound turns** (typically 0.1% to 1% of full scale).
- **Linearity:** Specified as a percentage of the **Total Resistance (R_total)**, often denoted as ±0.1% to ±1%.
- **Output Equation:** $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \left( \dfrac{R_x}{R_{total}} \right)$, where **R_x** is the resistance between the wiper and one end.

---

### 2.2 Encoders (Optical)

**Formal Definition:** An **encoder** is a digital position transducer that converts angular or linear position into a digital code, typically using optical, magnetic, or capacitive sensing of a patterned disc or strip.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of an odometer in a car. As the wheels turn, a digital counter increments. An optical encoder works the same way but uses light. A spinning glass or plastic disc has alternating transparent and opaque slots. A light-emitting diode (LED) on one side shines through these slots, and a photodiode on the other side counts the light pulses. By counting the pulses, the controller knows exactly how far the joint has turned.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incremental vs. Absolute Encoders:**
> - **Incremental Encoder:** Outputs a series of pulses (A and B channels, often in quadrature). Position is determined by *counting* pulses from a reference point. Loses absolute position on power-off.
> - **Absolute Encoder:** Outputs a unique binary (Gray code) word for every shaft angle. Retains absolute position even after power loss.

- **Resolution:** For a disc with **N** slots, angular resolution = $\dfrac{360^\circ}{N}$ (or $\dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ in radians).
- **Quadrature Decoding:** Provides 4x resolution by detecting rising and falling edges of two out-of-phase square waves (Channel A and Channel B).

---

### 2.3 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** The LVDT is a passive, inductive transducer that converts linear displacement into a corresponding AC voltage signal. It consists of a primary coil and two secondary coils wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable ferromagnetic core.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a treasure-hunting metal detector that beeps louder when metal gets closer to the coil. The LVDT has a metal slug (the core) sliding inside three wire coils. When the core is perfectly centered, the two outer coils receive equal magnetic "induction," and the output voltage is **zero**. As the core slides left, one coil gets more induced voltage than the other, producing a positive signal. Sliding right produces a negative signal. The *magnitude* tells the distance, and the *phase* tells the direction.

- **Operating Range:** Typically **±0.1 mm to ±50 cm**.
- **Excitation:** Requires an **AC excitation voltage** (typically 1 kHz to 10 kHz at 3 V to 15 V RMS).
- **Output Type:** Differential AC voltage, often demodulated to a unipolar DC voltage by a phase-sensitive demodulator circuit.

---

## 3. Velocity Sensor (Tachometer)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A velocity sensor (tachometer) is a transducer that provides an output signal directly proportional to the angular velocity (rpm) or linear velocity of a moving element.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Like the speedometer of a car, a tachometer tells the robot "how fast" a motor is spinning. In a DC tachometer, spinning a permanent magnet inside a coil generates a voltage (Faraday's Law) directly proportional to the rotational speed.

- **DC Tachometer:** Generates a DC voltage proportional to speed. Easy to interface but suffers from brush wear.
- **AC Tachometer (Resolver):** Two-phase output with frequency proportional to speed.
- **Optical Encoder as Velocity Sensor:** By measuring the time between successive encoder pulses, angular velocity $\omega$ can be computed.

---

## 4. Acceleration Sensor (Accelerometer)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** An accelerometer is a transducer that measures proper acceleration—the rate of change of velocity relative to free fall. Most modern accelerometers are **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** devices.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a small mass attached to a spring inside a sealed box, with a position sensor watching the mass. When you shake the box, the mass lags behind due to inertia, stretching the spring. The displacement of the mass (measured by the position sensor) tells you the acceleration. Modern MEMS chips use microscopic etched silicon structures that bend when accelerated, changing their capacitance.

- **Operating Principle:** Newton's Second Law: $F = m \cdot a$, sensed via mass displacement, capacitance change, or piezoelectric effect.
- **Units:** **g** (1 g = 9.81 m/s²) or m/s².
- **Types:** Capacitive MEMS, Piezoelectric, Piezoresistive.

---

## 5. Force, Pressure, and Torque Sensors

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** These sensors measure mechanical loading—force (linear push/pull, in Newtons), pressure (force per unit area, in Pascal), and torque (rotational force, in N·m)—typically using strain gauges bonded to a deformable elastic element.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Take a rubber band and stretch it. The further you stretch it, the harder you pull. A force sensor is essentially a calibrated metal spring with a strain gauge glued to it. As force is applied, the metal deforms microscopically, and the strain gauge's electrical resistance changes proportionally. The harder you push, the more the metal deforms, and the more the resistance changes.

- **Strain Gauge Equation:** $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$, where **G** is the gauge factor (typically ~2.0) and **ε** is the strain.
- **Wheatstone Bridge:** Four strain gauges arranged in a bridge circuit convert small resistance changes into measurable voltage.
- **Torque Sensor:** Often uses a hollow shaft with strain gauges arranged at ±45° to the shaft axis to measure shear strain.

---

## 6. Touch and Tactile Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A touch sensor detects binary contact (touch / no touch). A **tactile sensor** is more advanced, providing a spatial distribution of contact pressure across a surface, often in an array (matrix) form.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
A simple touch sensor is like a light switch—it is either pressed or not. A tactile sensor is like the skin on your fingertip. Your skin has thousands of tiny pressure receptors, and your brain forms a "pressure map" of what you are touching. Robotic tactile sensors work the same way: a matrix of small pressure-sensitive dots, each reporting how hard it is being pressed.

- **Technologies:** Piezoresistive, capacitive, optical (based on total internal reflection), and MEMS-based arrays.
- **Resolution:** Specified as number of **taxels** (tactile elements), e.g., a 16x16 array has 256 taxels.

---

## 7. Proximity Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A proximity sensor detects the presence of an object within a defined sensing zone *without physical contact*, typically using electromagnetic fields, light, or sound.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Like a bat using echolocation, a proximity sensor shouts out an invisible signal (light, infrared, ultrasound, or electromagnetic field) and listens for an echo. If something is nearby, the signal bounces back. By measuring the time-of-flight or the strength of the returned signal, the sensor knows how close an object is—or simply whether one is present.

- **Inductive Proximity:** Detects only metallic objects via eddy current losses in an EM field. Range: 1–50 mm.
- **Capacitive Proximity:** Detects both metallic and non-metallic (plastic, wood, liquid) by detecting changes in capacitance.
- **Ultrasonic Proximity:** Uses high-frequency sound waves (typically **40 kHz**). Range: 0.1 m to 10 m.
- **Infrared / Photoelectric:** Uses modulated light beams (visible or IR). Range: mm to 100+ m.
- **Hall Effect:** Detects magnetic fields; used for magnet-based proximity detection.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Question banks frequently test the *selection criterion* of a proximity sensor. Always mention the **target material**, **required range**, and **environmental conditions** when justifying a choice.

---

## 8. Summary of Sensor Classification

| Sensor | Type | Output | Typical Application in Robotics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Potentiometer | Analog, Contact | Voltage | Joint angle feedback (low-cost) |
| Optical Encoder | Digital, Contact | Pulses / Code | High-precision joint position |
| LVDT | Analog, Contactless | AC Voltage | Linear position of hydraulic pistons |
| Tachometer | Analog/Digital | Voltage / Frequency | Motor speed control |
| Accelerometer | Analog/Digital | Voltage / SPI/I2C | Vibration analysis, tilt sensing |
| Strain Gauge | Analog | Voltage (mV/V) | Force/torque at end-effector |
| Tactile Array | Analog Matrix | Pressure map | Grasping fragile objects |
| Proximity | Binary/Analog | Switching signal | Collision avoidance, part detection |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Potentiometer — Voltage Divider Theory

A linear potentiometer is fundamentally a **uniform resistive track** of total resistance $R_t$ with a sliding wiper. When an excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ is applied across the two end terminals, the wiper taps off a fraction of this voltage based on its position.

### Step-by-Step Working Principle:
1. The resistive element is a uniform film (carbon, cermet, or wire-wound) with resistance proportional to length: $R \propto L$.
2. A constant $V_{ex}$ (typically 5 V to 10 V DC) is applied across terminal 1 and terminal 3 (the ends).
3. The wiper at terminal 2 makes contact at a physical position $x$ from terminal 1.
4. The resistance between terminal 1 and the wiper is $R_x = R_t \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$, where $L$ is the total track length.
5. By the voltage divider rule, $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R_t}$.
6. Substituting: $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$.

### Sources of Error in Potentiometers:
- **Linearity Error:** Deviation of actual resistance profile from the ideal linear profile.
- **Resolution (Wire-Wound):** Limited by the discrete number of turns; the resistance changes in *steps* as the wiper moves from turn to turn.
- **Loading Error:** Caused by the finite input impedance of the measuring circuit.
- **Contact Resistance Variation:** Random noise from the wiper-track interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Potentiometers are widely used in the **RC servo motors** popular in educational robotics due to their simplicity, low cost, and absolute position feedback in a single turn. However, for high-precision industrial robots, they are replaced by encoders.

---

## 2. Optical Encoders — Disc Theory and Quadrature Decoding

### 2.1 Incremental Encoder Operation:
The encoder disc has a high number of precisely etched radial slots. A typical disc may have 100 to 10,000 lines per revolution.

- **Angular Resolution (mechanical):** $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N}$, where $N$ is the number of slots/lines.
- **Quadrature Resolution:** With 4x decoding (edges of A and B), effective resolution becomes $\theta_{eff} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{4N}$.

### 2.2 Absolute Encoder — Gray Code:
A disc with multiple concentric tracks. Each track has a different number of slots (powers of 2: 2, 4, 8, 16, ...). The pattern is arranged in **Gray code**, which ensures that *only one bit changes* between adjacent positions. This eliminates ambiguity during transition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Gray Code, Not Binary?** In straight binary, multiple bits can change at once during a transition. Due to sensor misalignment, the bits may not be read simultaneously, producing a transient false reading (e.g., 0011 → 0100 might briefly read as 0111 = 7, a huge error). Gray code prevents this catastrophic error.

### 2.3 Direction Discrimination (Quadrature):
Channel A and Channel B are two square waves **90° out of phase**.
- If A leads B by 90°: rotation is **clockwise**.
- If B leads A by 90°: rotation is **counter-clockwise**.

---

## 3. LVDT — Mutual Inductance and Phasor Analysis

### 3.1 Construction:
- **Primary Coil (P):** Centered on the former, energized by AC excitation $V_p$.
- **Secondary Coils (S1 and S2):** Identical, wound on either side of the primary, connected in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract).
- **Core:** A high-permeability ferromagnetic rod that moves inside the former.

### 3.2 Three Operating Positions:
1. **Null Position (Core Centered):** The flux linking S1 and S2 is equal. Their induced EMFs are equal in magnitude but **opposite in phase** (due to series-opposition connection). Net output: $V_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} = 0$.
2. **Core Displaced Toward S1:** $E_{S1} > E_{S2}$, producing a net output in-phase with the primary excitation.
3. **Core Displaced Toward S2:** $E_{S2} > E_{S1}$, producing a net output 180° out-of-phase with the primary excitation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase-Sensitive Demodulation:** The output AC voltage must be converted to DC. A demodulator circuit uses the phase relationship (in-phase or out-of-phase) to determine direction, and the amplitude to determine magnitude. The final DC output is bipolar: positive for one direction, negative for the other.

### 3.3 Sensitivity and Linearity:
- **Sensitivity (S):** $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta x}$, expressed in V/mm.
- For small displacements (typically within the central 50% of the stroke), the LVDT is highly linear.

---

## 4. Velocity Sensor — Tachogenerator Equation

A **DC tachogenerator** is essentially a small DC generator. Based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the induced EMF is:

$$E = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$$

where:
- $K$ = machine constant (depends on construction)
- $\phi$ = flux per pole (in Weber)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (in rad/s)

The output voltage is **linearly proportional** to the rotational speed, with polarity indicating direction. The sensitivity of a typical DC tachogenerator is in the range of **5 V to 50 V per 1000 rpm**.

---

## 5. Accelerometer — Newton's Second Law on a Proof Mass

The MEMS accelerometer consists of a proof mass suspended by compliant beams, with **differential capacitors** measuring its displacement.

### Working Steps:
1. When the sensor frame accelerates at $a$, the proof mass $m$ experiences an inertial force $F = -m \cdot a$ (opposite to the direction of acceleration).
2. This force deflects the proof mass by displacement $x$ against spring constant $k$ and damping $b$.
3. The mass is governed by: $m \ddot{x} + b \dot{x} + k x = -m a_{frame}$.
4. For steady-state acceleration (constant $a$), $x = -\dfrac{m a}{k}$.
5. The capacitance change is proportional to $x$, which is proportional to $a$.

> [!TIP]
> **Static vs. Dynamic Response:** A DC-coupled accelerometer can measure *constant* (static) acceleration like gravity, while an AC-coupled (piezoelectric) accelerometer can only measure *changing* acceleration (vibration) and cannot detect the 1 g gravitational bias.

---

## 6. Strain Gauge — Piezoresistive Theory

A **strain gauge** is a thin metallic foil arranged in a grid pattern, bonded to the surface of the test object.

### Governing Equation:
$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$$

where:
- $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ = fractional change in resistance
- $G$ = Gauge Factor (typically 2.0 for constantan, much higher for semiconductors)
- $\varepsilon$ = strain (dimensionless, often in microstrain, $\mu\varepsilon$)

### Wheatstone Bridge Circuit:
A single strain gauge cannot measure small strains accurately. Four gauges are arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge**:
- Two gauges are active (under tension/compression), two are dummy (for temperature compensation).
- The bridge is excited with $V_{ex}$ (typically 5 V to 10 V DC).
- Output: $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \left( \dfrac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \dfrac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} - \dfrac{\Delta R_4}{R_4} \right)$.

---

## 7. Touch, Tactile, and Proximity Sensors — Comparison

| Property | Touch Sensor | Tactile Sensor | Proximity Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output | Binary (0/1) | Pressure map (matrix) | Distance / Presence |
| Contact Required? | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Spatial Resolution | Single point | High (array) | Point or zone |
| Typical Tech | Mechanical switch, capacitive | Conductive rubber, FSR array | IR, ultrasonic, inductive |
| Use in Robots | Emergency stop, grip detection | Grasp force modulation | Obstacle detection |

---

## 8. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a quick-reference for KTU 2024 End Semester Examinations. Memorize the equations and the units.**

| Sensor / Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Potentiometer | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | $V$ in Volts, $x$ in m | Linear displacement to voltage |
| Wire-Wound Pot. Resolution | $\Delta x = \dfrac{L}{N}$ | $N$ = number of turns | Smallest measurable displacement |
| Encoder Resolution | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ | $N$ = slots/line count | Smallest measurable angle (rad) |
| Quadrature Resolution | $\theta_{eff} = \dfrac{2\pi}{4N}$ | — | Improved resolution by 4x |
| Tachometer EMF | $E = K \phi \omega$ | $K$ dimensionless, $\phi$ in Wb, $\omega$ in rad/s | Speed to voltage conversion |
| Strain Gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G$ dimensionless, $\varepsilon$ dimensionless | Strain to resistance change |
| Wheatstone Output | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot (\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4)$ | V | Strain to voltage |
| Accelerometer Mass Equation | $m \ddot{x} + b \dot{x} + kx = -m a$ | $m$ in kg, $k$ in N/m, $b$ in Ns/m | Dynamic response of proof mass |
| LVDT Output (Linear Range) | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | $K_{LVDT}$ in V/mm | Linear displacement to AC voltage |
| Ultrasonic Range | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $c \approx 343$ m/s, $t$ in s | Distance from time-of-flight |
| Force from Pressure | $F = P \cdot A$ | $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m² | Pressure to force conversion |
| Torque | $\tau = F \cdot r$ | $\tau$ in N·m, $r$ in m | Rotational force |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Potentiometer Output Voltage

**Problem Statement:** A linear potentiometer of total resistance 10 kΩ is used to measure the linear displacement of a robotic slide. The potentiometer is connected across a 10 V DC excitation. Find the output voltage when the slider is at 35% of the total stroke length.

### Given Data:
- $R_{total} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$
- $V_{ex} = 10 \text{ V}$
- Slider position $x = 0.35 \cdot L$

### Step 1: Identify the Principle
The potentiometer operates on the **voltage divider rule** of a series resistive network. The wiper divides the total resistance into two parts, and the output is the voltage across one part.

### Step 2: Express Resistance at the Wiper Position
Since the resistive track is uniform, the resistance is directly proportional to the physical length.

$$R_x = R_{total} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Given Values
$$R_x = 10{,}000 \cdot 0.35 = 3{,}500 \;\Omega = 3.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 4: Apply the Voltage Divider Equation
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R_{total}}$$

### Step 5: Substitute and Simplify
$$V_{out} = 10 \cdot \dfrac{3{,}500}{10{,}000} = 10 \cdot 0.35 = 3.5 \text{ V}$$

### Final Answer:
$$\boxed{V_{out} = 3.5 \text{ V}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Hack:** In KTU numericals, the percentage position can directly be multiplied by $V_{ex}$ since the potentiometer is linear. Here, 35% of 10 V = 3.5 V. This shortcut works only for *ideal linear* potentiometers.

---

## Derivation 2: Incremental Encoder Position from Pulse Count

**Problem Statement:** A robotic arm uses an incremental encoder with 2000 lines per revolution. The encoder is attached to a motor shaft. The controller counts 5000 pulses in 0.5 seconds, with 4x quadrature decoding enabled. Find: (a) Angular displacement, (b) Angular velocity.

### Given Data:
- $N = 2000$ lines/revolution
- Pulse count $C = 5000$
- Time $t = 0.5$ s
- Quadrature decoding = 4x

### Part (a): Angular Displacement

#### Step 1: Effective Resolution per Pulse
With 4x decoding, each line generates 4 countable edges.

$$N_{eff} = 4 \cdot N = 4 \cdot 2000 = 8000 \text{ counts per revolution}$$

#### Step 2: Angular Resolution per Count
$$\theta_{res} = \dfrac{2\pi}{N_{eff}} = \dfrac{2\pi}{8000} \text{ rad/count}$$

#### Step 3: Total Angular Displacement
$$\theta = C \cdot \theta_{res}$$

$$\theta = 5000 \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{8000}$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{5000 \cdot 2\pi}{8000} = \dfrac{5\pi}{4} \text{ rad}$$

$$\theta = 1.25\pi \approx 3.927 \text{ rad} \approx 225^\circ$$

### Part (b): Angular Velocity

#### Step 1: Compute Revolutions per Second
$$\text{Revolutions} = \dfrac{C}{N_{eff}} = \dfrac{5000}{8000} = 0.625 \text{ revolutions}$$

#### Step 2: Convert to Time
$$n = \dfrac{0.625 \text{ rev}}{0.5 \text{ s}} = 1.25 \text{ rev/s (Hz)}$$

#### Step 3: Convert to rad/s
$$\omega = 2\pi n = 2\pi \cdot 1.25 = 2.5\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega \approx 7.854 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Final Answer:
$$\boxed{\theta = 1.25\pi \text{ rad} \quad ; \quad \omega = 2.5\pi \text{ rad/s}}$$

---

## Derivation 3: LVDT Output Voltage Analysis

**Problem Statement:** An LVDT has the following characteristics:
- Primary excitation: 5 V RMS at 5 kHz
- Sensitivity: 0.5 V/mm
- Core displacement: +4 mm (towards S1)
Find: (a) Output RMS voltage, (b) Phase relationship with primary.

### Given Data:
- $V_{p} = 5 \text{ V RMS}$
- $S = 0.5 \text{ V/mm}$
- $x = +4 \text{ mm}$

### Step 1: Identify the Operating Region
The displacement is +4 mm, which is towards S1. The output is in-phase with the primary.

### Step 2: Compute the Output RMS Voltage
$$V_{out} = S \cdot x = 0.5 \cdot 4 = 2.0 \text{ V RMS}$$

### Step 3: Phase Relationship
Since the core moved towards S1 (positive direction), the differential output is **in-phase (0°)** with the primary excitation.

### Step 4: Demodulated DC Output
Assuming the demodulator has a gain of unity, the DC output is:

$$V_{DC} = 2.0 \text{ V (positive polarity)}$$

If the core were displaced $-4$ mm (towards S2), the output would be $V_{out} = 2.0$ V RMS but **180° out-of-phase**, and the demodulated DC would be $-2.0$ V.

### Final Answer:
$$\boxed{V_{out, RMS} = 2.0 \text{ V, In-phase with primary}}$$

---

## Derivation 4: DC Tachogenerator Output Voltage

**Problem Statement:** A DC tachogenerator has a constant field flux of 0.01 Wb and a machine constant of 50. The motor drives a robotic joint at 1500 rpm. Find: (a) Generated EMF, (b) Sensitivity in V per 1000 rpm.

### Given Data:
- $\phi = 0.01 \text{ Wb}$
- $K = 50$
- $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$

### Part (a): Generated EMF

#### Step 1: Convert rpm to rad/s
$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 1500}{60} = 50\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

#### Step 2: Apply the EMF Equation
$$E = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$$

$$E = 50 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 50\pi$$

$$E = 0.5 \cdot 50\pi = 25\pi \text{ V}$$

$$E \approx 78.54 \text{ V}$$

### Part (b): Sensitivity

#### Step 1: Voltage per 1000 rpm
$$V_{1000} = K \cdot \phi \cdot \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 1000}{60} = 50 \cdot 0.01 \cdot \dfrac{2000\pi}{60}$$

$$V_{1000} = 0.5 \cdot 104.72 = 52.36 \text{ V per 1000 rpm}$$

### Final Answer:
$$\boxed{E \approx 78.54 \text{ V} \quad ; \quad \text{Sensitivity} \approx 52.36 \text{ V/1000 rpm}}$$

---

## Derivation 5: Strain Gauge Force Measurement

**Problem Statement:** A load cell uses four strain gauges in a full Wheatstone bridge configuration, bonded to a steel cantilever of dimensions 100 mm × 20 mm × 5 mm. Young's modulus $E = 200$ GPa, gauge factor $G = 2.1$, bridge excitation $V_{ex} = 10$ V. A force $F = 500$ N is applied at the free end. Find: (a) Strain, (b) Bridge output voltage.

### Given Data:
- $L = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $b = 20 \text{ mm}$, $h = 5 \text{ mm}$
- $E = 200 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- $G = 2.1$, $V_{ex} = 10 \text{ V}$
- $F = 500 \text{ N}$

### Step 1: Bending Moment at the Cantilever Base
For a cantilever with load at the free end:
$$M = F \cdot L = 500 \cdot 0.1 = 50 \text{ N·m}$$

### Step 2: Section Modulus
$$Z = \dfrac{b h^2}{6} = \dfrac{0.02 \cdot (0.005)^2}{6} = \dfrac{0.02 \cdot 0.000025}{6}$$

$$Z = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{6} = 8.333 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 3: Bending Stress
$$\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{50}{8.333 \times 10^{-8}} = 6 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 600 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4: Strain (Hooke's Law)
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{6 \times 10^{8}}{200 \times 10^{9}} = 3 \times 10^{-3} = 3000 \; \mu\varepsilon$$

### Step 5: Full Bridge Output
For a full bridge, the output voltage is:
$$V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (1 + 1 + 1 + 1) = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon \cdot 4 = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$V_{out} = 10 \cdot 2.1 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-3} = 0.063 \text{ V} = 63 \text{ mV}$$

### Final Answer:
$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 3000 \; \mu\varepsilon \quad ; \quad V_{out} = 63 \text{ mV}}$$

---

## Derivation 6: Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor Distance

**Problem Statement:** An ultrasonic proximity sensor operates at 40 kHz. The speed of sound in air at 20°C is 343 m/s. The sensor emits a burst and detects the echo after 8.5 ms. Find the distance of the object.

### Given Data:
- $c = 343 \text{ m/s}$
- $t = 8.5 \text{ ms} = 8.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$

### Step 1: Total Distance Traveled by the Sound
$$d_{total} = c \cdot t = 343 \cdot 8.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$d_{total} = 2.9155 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2: Account for Round Trip
The sound travels from the sensor to the object *and* back. So the actual object distance is half:

$$d = \dfrac{d_{total}}{2} = \dfrac{2.9155}{2} = 1.458 \text{ m}$$

### Final Answer:
$$\boxed{d \approx 1.458 \text{ m}}$$

---

## Derivation 7: Accelerometer Sensitivity

**Problem Statement:** A MEMS accelerometer has a proof mass of $50 \; \mu g$ suspended by four beams of total stiffness $k = 0.1$ N/m. Find: (a) Static deflection under 1 g acceleration, (b) Sensitivity in terms of deflection per g.

### Given Data:
- $m = 50 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kg}$
- $k = 0.1 \text{ N/m}$
- $a = 1 g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### Step 1: Inertial Force
$$F = m \cdot a = 50 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 9.81 = 4.905 \times 10^{-7} \text{ N}$$

### Step 2: Static Deflection
For static equilibrium, $k \cdot x = F$, so:

$$x = \dfrac{F}{k} = \dfrac{4.905 \times 10^{-7}}{0.1} = 4.905 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 4.905 \; \mu\text{m}$$

### Step 3: Sensitivity
$$S = \dfrac{x}{g} = 4.905 \; \mu\text{m/g}$$

### Final Answer:
$$\boxed{x \approx 4.905 \; \mu\text{m per g}}$$

```mermaid
graph LR

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The physical quantity (e.g., position) is sensed by the transducer. The raw signal is amplified and filtered (signal conditioning), digitized by an ADC, processed by a microcontroller, and an actuator response is generated. The closed loop is complete when the actuator changes the physical state, which is again sensed by the transducer.


> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Channel A and Channel B are two square waves. The phase relationship between them determines the rotation direction. A leads B = clockwise; B leads A = counter-clockwise. The four edges (A rising, A falling, B rising, B falling) per cycle give 4x resolution in quadrature decoding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bridge Configuration:** In a *full bridge*, all four arms are strain gauges (two under tension, two under compression). In a *half bridge*, only two arms are active. A *quarter bridge* uses a single active gauge. Full bridge gives the highest sensitivity (4x) and best temperature compensation.


> [!NOTE]
> **Operation:** Under acceleration, the proof mass displaces. This changes the gap between interdigitated fingers, producing a differential capacitance change proportional to the applied acceleration. ASIC circuitry on-chip converts this to an analog voltage or digital (I²C/SPI) output.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define an LVDT. Explain how the direction of core displacement is detected from its output.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT) is a passive, inductive transducer that converts linear displacement into a corresponding AC voltage signal. It consists of one primary coil and two secondary coils wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable ferromagnetic core.

> **Direction Detection:** The two secondary coils are connected in *series opposition*. At the **null (center) position**, the induced EMFs in S1 and S2 are equal and opposite, giving a net output of 0 V. When the core moves towards S1, $E_{S1} > E_{S2}$, producing an output **in-phase** with the primary excitation. When the core moves towards S2, $E_{S2} > E_{S1}$, producing an output **180° out-of-phase** (anti-phase) with the primary. A **phase-sensitive demodulator** circuit interprets the phase to determine the direction of displacement and the amplitude to determine the magnitude.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three differences between incremental and absolute encoders.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Series of pulses (A, B) | Unique binary/Gray code word per position |
| Position on Power-Up | Unknown (must home) | Immediately known |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (more tracks) |
| Resolution | Set by line count | Set by number of tracks ($2^n$ positions for n tracks) |
| Code Pattern | N/A | Gray code used to avoid transition errors |

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** In ESE (End Semester Exam), Module 2 carries 14 marks. Students must answer *one* full question from Module 2 (with internal choice). The question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **LVDT**. Derive the expression for its output voltage and discuss its characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of an **optical incremental encoder** with quadrature decoding. A 2000-line encoder is used on a motor. If 8000 pulses are counted in 0.4 seconds with 4x decoding, find the angular velocity of the motor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) LVDT — Working Principle and Output Expression:**

**Construction:**
- A cylindrical former (non-magnetic) houses three coils.
- The **primary (P)** is centrally wound, energized by AC excitation (3–15 V RMS at 1–10 kHz).
- Two identical secondaries, **S1 and S2**, are wound on either side of the primary, connected in **series opposition**.
- A movable **ferromagnetic core** slides inside the former.

**Working (Three Positions):**

1. **Null Position:** Core is centered. The mutual inductance between P and S1 equals that between P and S2. The induced EMFs $E_{S1}$ and $E_{S2}$ are equal in magnitude but connected oppositely, so the net output is $V_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} = 0$.

2. **Core Displaced Towards S1:** The flux linking S1 increases. $E_{S1} > E_{S2}$, producing a net AC output that is **in-phase** with the primary excitation.

3. **Core Displaced Towards S2:** The flux linking S2 increases. $E_{S2} > E_{S1}$, producing a net AC output that is **180° out-of-phase** with the primary.

**Output Voltage Expression:**

$$V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$$

where $K_{LVDT}$ is the sensitivity (V/mm) and $x$ is the displacement (within the linear range, typically ±5 mm for typical LVDTs).

**Characteristics:**
- **High Linearity:** Within ±0.5% of full scale in the central region.
- **Infinite Resolution:** As an analog device, resolution is limited only by the measuring electronics, not by the transducer.
- **No Friction:** Core does not touch the coils → no mechanical wear, infinite mechanical life.
- **Sensitivity to Temperature:** Coil resistance changes with temperature; compensated by proper design.
- **Sensitivity to External EM Fields:** Magnetic shielding is required.

**(b) Optical Incremental Encoder:**

**Construction:**
- A glass/plastic disc with etched radial slots rotates with the shaft.
- An **LED** (light source) and a **photodiode/phototransistor** (detector) are placed on opposite sides of the disc.
- Two separate slot tracks produce **Channel A** and **Channel B** square waves, offset by 90° (in quadrature).

**Working:**
- As the disc rotates, the slots alternately transmit and block light.
- The photodetector produces a square wave whose frequency is proportional to rotational speed.
- **Quadrature Decoding:** Reading all four edges of A and B gives 4x the resolution of a single channel.
- **Direction:** If A leads B (90° ahead), rotation is clockwise; if B leads A, counter-clockwise.

**Numerical:**

- $N = 2000$ lines, 4x decoding, $C = 8000$ pulses in $t = 0.4$ s.
- Effective resolution: $N_{eff} = 4 \cdot 2000 = 8000$ counts/rev.
- Revolutions: $\dfrac{8000}{8000} = 1$ rev.
- Time: 0.4 s.
- $n = \dfrac{1}{0.4} = 2.5$ rev/s.
- $\omega = 2\pi n = 2\pi \cdot 2.5 = 5\pi$ rad/s $\approx 15.71$ rad/s.

$$\boxed{\omega = 5\pi \approx 15.71 \text{ rad/s}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **strain gauge-based load cell** with a Wheatstone bridge arrangement. A cantilever of dimensions 200 mm × 30 mm × 6 mm experiences a force of 1000 N at its free end. Given $E = 210$ GPa and $G = 2.0$, with a full bridge and 10 V excitation, find the output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **MEMS accelerometer** and derive the governing equation of motion of the proof mass. Distinguish between capacitive and piezoelectric accelerometers. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Strain Gauge Load Cell:**

**Working Principle:** A strain gauge is a thin metallic foil in a grid pattern, bonded to a deformable element (e.g., a cantilever beam). When the element deforms, the gauge stretches or compresses, changing its resistance. Four gauges in a **Wheatstone bridge** convert the small resistance change into a measurable voltage.

**Wheatstone Bridge Output:**
$$V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot (\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4)$$

For a **full bridge** (all four gauges active, two in tension, two in compression):
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Numerical:**

- $L = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m, $b = 30$ mm $= 0.03$ m, $h = 6$ mm $= 0.006$ m.
- $F = 1000$ N, $E = 210 \times 10^9$ Pa, $G = 2.0$, $V_{ex} = 10$ V.

**Bending moment at the base:**
$$M = F \cdot L = 1000 \cdot 0.2 = 200 \text{ N·m}$$

**Section modulus:**
$$Z = \dfrac{b h^2}{6} = \dfrac{0.03 \cdot (0.006)^2}{6} = \dfrac{0.03 \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{-5}}{6} = 1.8 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

**Bending stress:**
$$\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{200}{1.8 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.111 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} = 1111.11 \text{ MPa}$$

**Strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{1.111 \times 10^9}{210 \times 10^9} = 5.291 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Output voltage (full bridge):**
$$V_{out} = 10 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 5.291 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1058 \text{ V} \approx 105.8 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 105.8 \text{ mV}}$$

**(b) MEMS Accelerometer:**

**Construction:** A MEMS accelerometer consists of a microscopic **proof mass** suspended by compliant **beams** within a silicon substrate. The mass is surrounded by **interdigitated finger capacitors** that detect its position.

**Working:** When the sensor frame accelerates, the proof mass (due to inertia) deflects relative to the frame. This deflection changes the gap of the differential capacitors. The capacitance change is proportional to the displacement, which in turn is proportional to the applied acceleration.

**Equation of Motion (Derivation):**
Let $x$ be the displacement of the proof mass relative to the sensor frame, $m$ the mass, $b$ the damping coefficient, and $k$ the spring constant of the suspension. Applying Newton's second law in the non-inertial frame:

$$m \ddot{x} + b \dot{x} + k x = -m a_{frame}$$

For a constant (static) acceleration, $\ddot{x} = \dot{x} = 0$:

$$x = -\dfrac{m a_{frame}}{k}$$

**Capacitive vs Piezoelectric Accelerometer:**

| Property | Capacitive (MEMS) | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| Output Type | Analog voltage or digital | Charge / voltage |
| Static (DC) Response | Yes (DC-coupled) | No (AC-coupled only) |
| Frequency Range | Low to medium (DC to ~5 kHz) | High (0.1 Hz to 50 kHz) |
| Sensitivity | Medium | High |
| Typical Use | Tilt, motion, navigation | Vibration analysis |
| Power | Requires excitation | Self-generating |

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PCMRT601:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always write the unit after every numerical value. Writing "5" without "V" or "mm" may be penalized.
> 2. **LVDT Null Position:** Many students incorrectly state that the LVDT output is "zero" without explaining the *series opposition* of the secondary coils. Examiners expect a clear phasor explanation.
> 3. **Encoder Resolution:** Confusing *mechanical resolution* (lines per revolution) with *effective resolution* (counts per revolution with 4x decoding). State both clearly.
> 4. **Strain Gauge Bridge:** Forgetting to specify whether the configuration is quarter, half, or full bridge. The output equation differs by a factor of 4 between these.
> 5. **Tachogenerator vs Encoder:** The DC tachogenerator gives an *analog* voltage; using pulses per second from an encoder is a separate method. Do not mix the two.
> 6. **Proximity Sensor Selection:** Always justify the choice by *target material*, *range requirement*, and *environment* (dust, light, temperature). A bare statement like "use ultrasonic" without justification loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Static vs Dynamic Response of Accelerometers:** A piezoelectric accelerometer cannot measure constant gravity (1 g). MEMS capacitive accelerometers can. This is a frequent ESE question.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Module 2 Sensors**

- [ ] **Potentiometer:** Output $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot (x/L)$. Issues: contact wear, limited resolution (wire-wound), linearity error.
- [ ] **Optical Encoder:** Incremental (pulses, A and B quadrature, 4x decoding). Absolute (Gray code, multi-track). Resolution = $360°/N$ or $360°/(4N)$.
- [ ] **LVDT:** Primary at center, two secondaries in series opposition. Null position → 0 V output. Core displacement → output in-phase or anti-phase. Linear range ≈ ±5 mm typical. High resolution, contactless, infinite mechanical life.
- [ ] **Tachogenerator:** $E = K \phi \omega$. DC tachogenerator output is linear with speed. Optical encoder can also be used to compute velocity by pulse timing.
- [ ] **Accelerometer:** $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = -ma$. Static deflection $x = ma/k$. MEMS (capacitive, DC-coupled) vs Piezoelectric (AC-coupled, vibration only).
- [ ] **Strain Gauge:** $\Delta R/R = G\varepsilon$. Full bridge gives $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$ (4x sensitivity). Typical gauge factor ~2.0 for metallic gauges.
- [ ] **Force / Pressure / Torque:** Force = Pressure × Area. Torque = Force × Lever arm. All use strain gauge principle in load cells.
- [ ] **Touch vs Tactile:** Touch is binary (contact/no-contact). Tactile is a pressure map (matrix of taxels). Use in robotic grasping and delicate manipulation.
- [ ] **Proximity Sensor:** Inductive (metals only, short range), Capacitive (any material), Ultrasonic (long range, sound-based, $d = ct/2$), Infrared/Photoelectric (light beam, longest range), Hall Effect (magnetic).
- [ ] **Hall Effect Sensor:** Used to detect magnetic fields; common in BLDC motor commutation and magnet-based position sensing.
- [ ] **Sensors in Robotics:** Proprioceptive (internal) vs Exteroceptive (external). Position, velocity, acceleration are proprioceptive. Touch, tactile, proximity, vision are exteroceptive.
- [ ] **Gray Code Advantage:** Only one bit changes between adjacent codes → eliminates transition errors in absolute encoders.
- [ ] **Quadrature Decoding:** Gives 4x resolution by counting all four edges (rising/falling) of A and B channels.
- [ ] **LVDT Demodulation:** Phase-sensitive demodulator converts AC bipolar signal to DC bipolar signal. Phase → direction, amplitude → magnitude.
- [ ] **Wheatstone Bridge Configurations:** Quarter (1 active gauge, sensitivity 1x), Half (2 active, 2x), Full (4 active, 4x).
- [ ] **Selection Rule of Thumb:** For high-precision digital → Encoder. For contactless linear → LVDT. For low-cost analog → Potentiometer. For vibration → Piezoelectric accelerometer. For tilt/motion → MEMS accelerometer. For force → Strain gauge load cell. For presence detection → Proximity (technology chosen by material and range).
- [ ] **Common Units:** Position in m or rad; Velocity in m/s or rad/s; Acceleration in m/s² or g (1 g = 9.81 m/s²); Force in N; Pressure in Pa; Torque in N·m; Gauge factor dimensionless; Sensitivity in V/mm or V/g.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — PCMRT601 Robotics & Machine Vision (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Range and Sniff Sensor, Robot End Effectors, Types of End Effectors, Mechanical Gripper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT601 - ROBOTICS & MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Range and Sniff Sensor, Robot End Effectors, Types of End Effectors, Mechanical Gripper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Range Sensors

A **Range Sensor** (also called a Distance Sensor or Proximity Sensor) is a non-contact (or contact-based) sensory device mounted on a robotic manipulator or mobile platform that measures the distance between the sensor and an object in its workspace. Range sensors provide the robot with spatial awareness, enabling obstacle avoidance, path planning, object localization, and feedback for compliant motion control.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Range sensors are categorized under *internal sensors* when measuring robot's own joint/motor parameters (e.g., encoders, resolvers), and under *external sensors* when measuring environmental variables (e.g., ultrasonic, laser rangefinder, IR, capacitive). For Module 2, the focus is predominantly on **external range sensors**.

**Classification of Range Sensors:**

| Category | Working Principle | Typical Range |
|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| Contact (Whisker/Bumper) | Mechanical deflection | 0–5 mm |
| Ultrasonic | Time-of-Flight of sound waves | **30 mm – 10 m** |
| Infrared (Triangulation) | Reflected IR light angle | 20–1500 mm |
| Laser Time-of-Flight (LiDAR) | Reflected laser pulse timing | 0.1 m – 200 m |
| Laser Triangulation | Geometric light triangulation | 10–1000 mm |
| Capacitive | Change in electric field | 1–50 mm |
| Inductive (Magnetic) | Eddy current formation | 1–80 mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Range Sensor as a Bat:** Think of a **bat** navigating a dark cave. It emits high-frequency sound waves (ultrasonic pulses) and listens for the echo. By measuring how long the echo takes to return, the bat calculates the exact distance to a wall or prey. An ultrasonic range sensor operates on the **exact same physical principle**: emit–reflect–time–distance.

### 1.2 Sniff Sensors

A **Sniff Sensor** (also called a **Chemical Sensor**, **Electronic Nose (e-Nose)**, or **Gas Sensor**) is a device that detects and identifies chemical compounds, gases, or vapors in the environment. In robotics, sniff sensors equip autonomous robots with **olfactory perception**, allowing them to perform tasks like hazardous gas detection, mine rescue operations, environmental monitoring, drug/explosive detection, and food-quality inspection.

**Classification of Sniff Sensors:**

| Type | Detection Principle |
|------|---------------------|
| **Metal-Oxide Semiconductor (MOS)** | Resistance change of SnO₂ on gas adsorption |
| **Electrochemical** | Redox reaction producing measurable current |
| **Infrared (NDIR)** | Gas-specific IR absorption spectrum |
| **Photoionization Detector (PID)** | UV ionization of gas molecules |
| **Conducting Polymer** | Swelling alters polymer resistance |
| **Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM)** | Mass change shifts resonant frequency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Sniff Sensor as a Trained Sniffer Dog:** A sniffer dog at an airport smells a suitcase and alerts its handler when it detects drugs or explosives. An **electronic nose** does the same job: an array of chemical sensors (like olfactory receptors in a dog's nose) is exposed to air, each sensor responds uniquely to different molecules, and a **pattern recognition algorithm** (the dog's brain) classifies the smell into "safe" or "dangerous."

### 1.3 Robot End Effectors

A **Robot End Effector** (also called **End-of-Arm Tooling (EOAT)**, **Robot Tool**, or **Manipulator Hand**) is the device physically attached to the robot's wrist (end of the kinematic chain) that interacts directly with the workpiece or environment to perform the intended task. It is the **only physical interface** between the robot and the world.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** "An end effector is a device or tool specifically attached to the robot's wrist that enables the robot to perform a particular task, such as grasping, welding, painting, drilling, or sensing."

**Conceptual Analogy — End Effector as a Human Hand:** Consider the human arm. The brain (controller) sends signals through the nerves (wires), the muscles (actuators) move the bones (links), but it's the **hand** at the end that picks up the cup, types the keyboard, or holds the pen. A robot's **end effector** is its "hand" — without it, the arm is useless.

### 1.4 Mechanical Gripper (Preview)

A **Mechanical Gripper** is a specialized type of end effector that uses **mechanical fingers (jaws)** to grasp, hold, and release objects through physical contact and applied force. It is the **most widely used** end effector in industrial robotics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Range Sensor — Deep Analysis

### 2.1.1 Ultrasonic Range Sensor — Time-of-Flight Principle

An ultrasonic sensor emits a high-frequency sound pulse (typically **40 kHz**, above human hearing). The pulse travels through air, strikes an object, and reflects back. The sensor's receiver detects the echo, and the embedded circuitry measures the round-trip time *t*.

**Operational Sequence:**
1. **Emission:** Piezoelectric transducer emits a **40 kHz** burst.
2. **Propagation:** Sound travels at speed *c* in air (≈ **343 m/s** at 20 °C).
3. **Reflection:** Obstacle reflects a portion of the wave back.
4. **Detection:** Receiver transducer converts the reflected wave to a voltage pulse.
5. **Computation:** The microcontroller computes distance using the time difference.

**Effect of Temperature:** Sound velocity in air depends on temperature:
$$c = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad [\text{m/s}]$$
where *T* is the air temperature in °C.

**Advantages:** Cheap, simple, works in dust/smoke, detects transparent objects.
**Disadvantages:** Poor angular resolution, slow update rate, specular reflections from smooth surfaces.

### 2.1.2 Infrared (IR) Triangulation Sensor

A focused beam of IR light is projected onto the target. The reflected light hits a position-sensitive detector (PSD) at an angle. As the target moves, the spot on the PSD shifts, and the controller calculates distance via **geometric triangulation**.

### 2.1.3 Laser Range Finder (LiDAR)

Uses a **laser beam** instead of sound. Two variants:
- **Time-of-Flight LiDAR:** Directly measures the round-trip time of a laser pulse.
- **Phase-Shift LiDAR:** Measures the phase difference between emitted and reflected continuous-wave laser signals.
- **Triangulation LiDAR:** Like IR triangulation but with laser precision.

## 2.2 Sniff Sensor — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Metal-Oxide Semiconductor (MOS) Sensor

The active material is a thin film of **tin dioxide (SnO₂)** heated to ~300–400 °C. When target gas molecules adsorb on the surface, electron transfer occurs, changing the film's electrical resistance. A load resistor converts this resistance change to a measurable voltage.

**Resistance Equation:**
$$\frac{R_s}{R_0} = \left(\frac{C}{X^{\beta}}\right)$$
where $R_s$ = sensor resistance in gas, $R_0$ = resistance in clean air, $X$ = gas concentration (ppm), and $\beta$ is a sensor-specific exponent.

### 2.2.2 Electronic Nose Architecture
