# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Visual Servoing**
> The **#1 mistake** is confusing the **interaction matrix** $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}$ with the **robot Jacobian** $\mathbf{J}_{geom}$. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Remember:
> - $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}$ : image → camera velocity
> - $\mathbf{J}_{geom}$ : joint → camera velocity
> - $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}} \mathbf{J}_{geom}$ : image → joint velocity
>
> The second common error is forgetting the **depth $Z$** in $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{p}}$ — without $Z$, the control is dimensionally inconsistent and unstable. A third trap is **feature singularity** when features become co-linear or occluded, causing $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}$ to lose rank.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Position-Based Visual Servoing (PBVS) and Image-Based Visual Servoing (IBVS). List two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | PBVS | IBVS |
|--------|------|------|
| **Control Space** | 3-D Cartesian pose | 2-D image feature |
| **Reconstruction** | Explicit 3-D pose required | No reconstruction |
| **Sensitivity to calibration** | High (camera & robot) | Low |
| **Trajectory in image** | Not controlled | Straight lines |
| **Stability region** | Larger | Smaller (local minima) |

**Two advantages — IBVS:** (i) Robust to camera calibration errors since control is in image plane. (ii) Direct control over image features prevents object leaving field-of-view. **(1.5 marks)**

**Two advantages — PBVS:** (i) Straightforward trajectory in Cartesian space. (ii) Decoupled control of translation and rotation. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the Interaction Matrix? Write the interaction matrix for a point feature in image-based visual servoing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The interaction matrix $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}$ (or image Jacobian) is a matrix that linearly relates the time-derivative of an image feature vector $\mathbf{s}$ to the camera's spatial velocity $\mathbf{v}_c$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{s}} = \mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}} \, \mathbf{v}_c
$$

**Matrix (1.5 marks):** For a point feature $(x, y)$ with depth $Z$:

$$
\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{p}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
-\frac{1}{Z} & 0 & \frac{x}{Z} & x y & -(1+x^2) & y \\
0 & -\frac{1}{Z} & \frac{y}{Z} & 1+y^2 & -x y & -x
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — IBVS & Interaction Matrix [Preferred for Module-4]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the interaction matrix for a point feature in image-based visual servoing. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the interaction matrix, derive the IBVS velocity control law that ensures exponential convergence of image features. Show that the closed-loop system is locally exponentially stable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Pinhole camera model with normalized coordinates.
2. Camera moves with linear velocity $\mathbf{v}$ and angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ in a static scene.
3. Image features are points with known (or estimated) depth $Z$.

**Perspective projection (1 mark):**

$$
x = \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = \frac{Y}{Z}
$$

**Time differentiation (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{x} = \frac{\dot{X}}{Z} - \frac{X \dot{Z}}{Z^2}, \quad \dot{y} = \frac{\dot{Y}}{Z} - \frac{Y \dot{Z}}{Z^2}
$$

**Camera kinematic relation (1.5 marks):**

$$
\dot{\mathbf{P}}_c = -\mathbf{v} - \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{P}_c
$$

Expanding:

$$
\dot{X} = -v_x - \omega_y Z + \omega_z Y
$$

$$
\dot{Y} = -v_y - \omega_z X + \omega_x Z
$$

$$
\dot{Z} = -v_z - \omega_x Y + \omega_y X
$$

**Substitution and matrix form (2 marks):** Substituting $X = xZ$ and $Y = yZ$ into the projection derivatives and collecting terms in $\mathbf{v}_c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= -\frac{v_x}{Z} + \frac{x v_z}{Z} + x y \omega_x - (1+x^2) \omega_y + y \omega_z
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{y} &= -\frac{v_y}{Z} + \frac{y v_z}{Z} + (1+y^2) \omega_x - x y \omega_y - x \omega_z
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{p}} \,
\begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ v_z \\ \omega_x \\ \omega_y \\ \omega_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

with $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{p}}$ as the standard $2 \times 6$ matrix shown in §2.2.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The interaction matrix depends on the current image coordinates $(x, y)$ and the depth $Z$ — accurate depth estimation is therefore essential for IBVS stability.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Define error (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{e}(t) = \mathbf{s}(t) - \mathbf{s}^*
$$

**Step 2 — Error dynamics (1 mark):** Differentiating and applying the kinematic model:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{e}} = \dot{\mathbf{s}} = \mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}} \, \mathbf{v}_c
$$

**Step 3 — Impose desired dynamics (1 mark):** For exponential stability with rate $\lambda > 0$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{e}} = -\lambda \, \mathbf{e}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for control input (1.5 marks):**

$$
\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}} \, \mathbf{v}_c = -\lambda \, \mathbf{e} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{v}_c = -\lambda \, \mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}^{-1} \, \mathbf{e}
$$

If $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}$ is rectangular, use the Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}^{+}$.

**Step 5 — Practical control law (1 mark):** Since the true $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}$ requires perfect knowledge of $Z$ and calibration, we use an estimate:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{v}_c = -\lambda \, \widehat{\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}}^{+} \, (\mathbf{s} - \mathbf{s}^*)}
$$

**Step 6 — Stability proof (1.5 marks):** Substituting back:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{e}} = \mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}} \, \mathbf{v}_c = -\lambda \, \mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}} \, \widehat{\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}}^{+} \, \mathbf{e}
$$

If $\widehat{\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}} = \mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}$ (perfect model), then $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}} \, \mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}^{+} = \mathbf{I}$, giving:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{e}} = -\lambda \, \mathbf{e} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{e}(t) = \mathbf{e}(0) \, e^{-\lambda t}
$$

This shows **global exponential stability** of the regulation task. With model errors, the system remains **locally exponentially stable** in a neighbourhood where $\widehat{\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}}$ is a sufficiently close estimate of $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — PBVS & Hybrid 2½-D Servoing [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain Position-Based Visual Servoing (PBVS). Derive the PBVS control law. Compare its stability with IBVS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the 2½-D visual servoing scheme? Explain how it overcomes the limitations of PBVS and IBVS. Write its control law in terms of the partial-pose and image-feature errors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**PBVS Definition (1 mark):** In PBVS, the vision system estimates the **3-D Cartesian pose** $\mathbf{T}_c^{o}$ of the target object with respect to the camera, and the controller minimizes the error between the current and desired camera poses in SE(3).

**Pose representation (1.5 marks):** The pose error is decomposed into translation $\mathbf{t} - \mathbf{t}^*$ and rotation (angle–axis $\theta \mathbf{u}$):

$$
\mathbf{e} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{t}^* \\ \theta \mathbf{u} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Control law (2 marks):** The error dynamics in the camera frame is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{e}} = \begin{bmatrix} -\mathbf{I} & [\theta \mathbf{u}]_{\times} \\ 0 & \mathbf{L}_{\omega}^{-1} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{v}_c
$$

For small errors, the cross-coupling is negligible, and the **PBVS control law** simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{v}_c = -\lambda \, \mathbf{e}}
$$

**Comparison with IBVS (2.5 marks):**

| Property | PBVS | IBVS |
|----------|------|------|
| **Stability region** | Large, global (small errors) | Local; local minima possible |
| **Image trajectory** | Curved, may leave FOV | Straight, stays in FOV |
| **Calibration sensitivity** | High (camera + robot) | Low (mostly camera intrinsics) |
| **Reconstruction required** | Yes (3-D pose) | No |
| **Coupling translation-rotation** | Yes (in $\mathbf{e}$) | No (decoupled in $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}$) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — PBVS Decoupling:** Students often claim PBVS decouples translation and rotation. The control law $\mathbf{v}_c = -\lambda \mathbf{e}$ is *not* decoupled in general because the rotation part $\theta \mathbf{u}$ couples with translation in SE(3). Only the *desired* dynamics is decoupled.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Definition of 2½-D Visual Servoing (2 marks):** Introduced by **Malis, Chaumette & Boudet (1999)**, the 2½-D scheme uses a feature vector containing **partial 3-D information** (the translation component and the rotation $\theta \mathbf{u}$ from current to desired pose) **and** 2-D image features. Hence "2½-D": two dimensions in image plane plus half a dimension from partial 3-D.

**Feature vector (2 marks):**

$$
\mathbf{s} = \begin{bmatrix} (\mathbf{t}_c - \mathbf{t}_{c^*}) / \delta \\ \theta \mathbf{u} \\ \rho \end{bmatrix}
$$

where:
- $(\mathbf{t}_c - \mathbf{t}_{c^*}) / \delta$ is the normalized translation error
- $\theta \mathbf{u}$ is the rotation error (angle–axis)
- $\rho$ is the **scaled image feature** $\rho = \frac{s}{\delta}$ with $\delta$ being the **estimated depth scale factor** $\delta = Z^* / Z$

**Control law (1.5 marks):** Decoupled exponential convergence:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{v}_c = -\lambda \,
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & 0 \\ 0 & \mathbf{I} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} (\mathbf{t}_c - \mathbf{t}_{c^*}) / \delta \\ \theta \mathbf{u} \\ \rho \end{bmatrix}}
$$

**Advantages over IBVS and PBVS (1.5 marks):**
1. **No 3-D model required** (like IBVS).
2. **Globally stable** (like PBVS) and decoupled in translation/rotation.
3. **Image trajectory** is approximately straight, avoiding the FOV-exit problem of PBVS.
4. **Robust to camera calibration errors** of the rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Visual Servoing (KTU Module 4)**
>
> ✅ Visual servoing = closed-loop control using image features.
>
> ✅ **PBVS** controls in 3-D pose; **IBVS** controls in 2-D image; **Hybrid/2½-D** combines both.
>
> ✅ The **Interaction Matrix** $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{p}}$ (2×6) for a point feature depends on $(x, y, Z)$.
>
> ✅ IBVS control law: $\mathbf{v}_c = -\lambda \, \widehat{\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}}^{+} \, (\mathbf{s} - \mathbf{s}^*)$.
>
> ✅ PBVS control law: $\mathbf{v}_c = -\lambda \, \mathbf{e}$ where $\mathbf{e}$ is the pose error in SE(3).
>
> ✅ Desired error dynamics: $\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \lambda \mathbf{e} = 0$ ⇒ **exponential stability**.
>
> ✅ **Eye-in-Hand** = camera on gripper; **Eye-to-Hand** = camera fixed in workspace.
>
> ✅ Joint velocity from image: $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = -\lambda \left(\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}} \mathbf{J}_{geom}\right)^{-1} \mathbf{e}$.
>
> ✅ **Task function** $\mathbf{e}$ is a $k$-D vector with full-rank interaction matrix $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{e}}$.
>
> ✅ Depth $Z$ is the **key unknown** in IBVS — often obtained via stereo, depth sensor, or known object model.
>
> ✅ **IBVS** advantage: robust to calibration. **PBVS** advantage: globally stable, simple trajectory. **2½-D** = best of both.
>
> ✅ Common pitfalls: confusing $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{s}}$ with $\mathbf{J}_{geom}$; ignoring depth $Z$; rank-deficient feature sets; FOV-exit in PBVS.
>
> ✅ Camera velocity vector: $\mathbf{v}_c = (v_x, v_y, v_z, \omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z)^T$ — **6 components** for 6-DoF.
>
> ✅ Control gain $\lambda$ is typically chosen in the range $0.1$ to $1.0$ for stable visual servoing at 30–60 Hz frame rate.

---
